cross_system.title: Cross-system signal: credit_card flow softening ahead of stock
cross_system.body: Credit card spending is slowing down, which usually happens before credit card loans start shrinking. When people spend less on their cards, they typically pay down their balances over the next few months, causing total credit card debt to drop. This gives banks a few months' head start to see what's coming.
cross_system.implication: Banks should prepare for lower credit card loan growth and adjust their lending plans before the slowdown hits their loan books.
chain: People are spending less money on their credit cards
Lower spending usually leads to people paying down their card balances over the next few months
Banks can spot this early and adjust their credit card lending before loan growth actually slows

cross_system.title: Unsecured retail credit appetite — expanding (5/5 measurements observed)
cross_system.body: Personal loans for credit cards and other purposes grew 3.78 percent and 12.7 percent year-on-year, while credit card balances outstanding rose 8.15 percent. Consumer durable loans fell 3.38 percent. Most unsecured lending is expanding, meaning banks are willing to lend more to people without collateral because they see demand and acceptable risk.
cross_system.implication: A bank should grow its unsecured personal loan book now, especially in the other personal loan category growing at 12.7 percent, while watching credit quality closely since these loans have no asset backing.
chain: Banks are lending more personal loans without collateral, with growth of 3.78 percent and 12.7 percent in two categories
People are borrowing more on credit cards too, with outstanding balances up 8.15 percent, showing steady demand for unsecured credit
This is a good time to lend in these categories, but the bank must monitor repayment carefully because there is no property or gold to recover if borrowers default

basis.headline: Unsecured retail credit appetite → expanding
chain: Measured inputs: Credit Card Outstanding +3.8% YoY, Other Personal Loans +12.7% YoY, Consumer Durables -3.4% YoY, Credit card spend falling, Credit cards outstanding +8.2% YoY.
Member directions combine — measures and proxies count as-is, contra-indicators flipped — giving an expanding read on 5 of 5 measurements.

The willingness of households to carry — and lenders to extend — unsecured retail credit. No pipeline measures it directly: SIBC measures the unsecured stock (credit-card outstanding, other personal loans, consumer durables), payments measures the flow (credit-card spend) and reach (cards in force). Together they bound the construct: when stock, flow and reach move together the appetite read is unambiguous; when they diverge, the divergence itself is the signal.
Direction sustained across two composed periods → status active.

cross_system.title: Card-based consumption activity — expanding (5/5 measurements observed)
cross_system.body: Card terminals grew 1.45 percent, vehicle loans jumped 17.97 percent, but consumer durable loans fell 3.38 percent. People are borrowing heavily to buy cars and trucks, using cards a bit more, yet pulling back on financing fridges and TVs. The pattern says households are spending selectively—big-ticket transport yes, home goods no.
cross_system.implication: A bank should push auto finance hard, keep card offers steady, and avoid chasing consumer-durable portfolios until that 3.38 percent decline reverses.
chain: Card infrastructure is growing at 1.45 percent and vehicle lending is surging at 17.97 percent, so people are transacting more and financing expensive transport.
At the same time consumer durable lending dropped 3.38 percent, meaning households are skipping loans for appliances and electronics.
Banks should allocate capital to auto loans where demand is strong and wait on durable-goods credit until appetite returns.

basis.headline: Card-based consumption activity → expanding
chain: Measured inputs: Credit card spend falling, Debit card spend falling, POS terminals +1.4% YoY, Consumer Durables -3.4% YoY, Vehicle Loans +18.0% YoY.
Member directions combine — measures and proxies count as-is, contra-indicators flipped — giving an expanding read on 5 of 5 measurements.

Household consumption as expressed through card rails. Payments measures the transaction flows (credit + debit card spend) directly; acceptance infrastructure (POS terminals) proxies the merchant side of the same activity; SIBC's consumer-durable and vehicle loan stocks proxy the credit-financed share of it.
Direction sustained across two composed periods → status active.

cross_system.title: Unsecured spend–stock cycle — partially engaged (3/3 segments live)
cross_system.body: This is a feedback cycle read across both pipelines: spending, balances, and appetite feed each other. The basis below shows each segment and whether it is currently running with the cycle or against it.
cross_system.implication: A fully-running cycle compounds; a segment moving against it is the early warning that the cycle is turning. Watch the reversed segment.
chain: Segment states: Unsecured retail credit appetite → Credit card spend (value) — running; Credit card spend (value) → Credit Card Outstanding — running in reverse; Credit Card Outstanding → Unsecured retail credit appetite — running.
A loop fires only when every segment runs in its expected direction; 3 of 3 are live, so the cycle is engaged but not aligned — one segment is moving against the cycle.

Appetite expresses as card spend (flow), spend accumulates into card balances (stock), and growing balances evidence the appetite that started the cycle. The loop fires only when all three segments move the same way — a clean composed read that the unsecured retail cycle is running (or, reversed, unwinding). Segments span both pipelines plus the confirmed flow→stock cross-edge.

basis.headline: Unsecured spend–stock cycle → partial

members.label: Unsecured retail credit appetite → Credit card spend (value)
Credit card spend (value) → Credit Card Outstanding
Credit Card Outstanding → Unsecured retail credit appetite

chain: Segment states: Unsecured retail credit appetite → Credit card spend (value) — running; Credit card spend (value) → Credit Card Outstanding — running in reverse; Credit Card Outstanding → Unsecured retail credit appetite — running.
A loop fires only when every segment runs in its expected direction; 3 of 3 are live, so the cycle is engaged but not aligned — one segment is moving against the cycle.

Appetite expresses as card spend (flow), spend accumulates into card balances (stock), and growing balances evidence the appetite that started the cycle. The loop fires only when all three segments move the same way — a clean composed read that the unsecured retail cycle is running (or, reversed, unwinding). Segments span both pipelines plus the confirmed flow→stock cross-edge.

sibc.title: Co-lending & warehouse-financing infrastructure
sibc.body: Small business credit is growing at 30.09 percent this year and now makes up 23.12 percent of all business lending. Growth in micro and small business lending jumped by 23.77 percentage points, driven by government systems like UDYAM registration and GST data that make it easier to check if tiny businesses are real and creditworthy. Banks cannot reach thousands of small borrowers alone, so they need partners who can do the ground work of finding customers and verifying them.
sibc.implication: The bank should build partnerships with fintech companies and NBFCs that specialize in reaching small businesses, using these partners to handle customer acquisition and initial checks while the bank provides the funding.
chain: Government systems like UDYAM and GST now create digital records for tiny businesses that were previously invisible
These records let lenders verify small borrowers quickly, making micro-lending suddenly viable at scale
Banks lack the staff and local presence to serve thousands of small customers directly, so they must work through specialized partners who handle the on-ground operations
sibc._driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: Gold loan market entry / deepening for banks
sibc.body: Banks are growing gold loans by 121.21 percent compared to last year, and this growth has been climbing for 10.0 periods in a row. Gold prices jumped sharply between 2023 and 2026, so customers can now borrow much more money using the same gold jewelry or coins as security. Banks are winning customers from finance companies because they have local branches where staff can check the gold is real and store it safely, plus they charge lower interest rates.
sibc.implication: Banks should open more gold loan counters in branches, train staff to test gold quickly, and advertise lower rates to pull customers from finance companies while gold prices stay high.
chain: Gold prices rose sharply from 2023 to 2026, so the same piece of jewelry now backs a bigger loan.
Banks have trusted branches and lower rates, making customers switch from finance companies.
As long as gold stays expensive, banks can lend more per gram and grow this business fast.

sibc.title: MSME origination infrastructure investment window
sibc.body: Small business lending is growing at 30.09 pct per year, and these tiny businesses now make up 23.12 pct of all MSME lending. Their growth rate jumped by 23.77 pp recently, reaching 32.68 pct, while bigger MSME borrowers are growing 20.06 pp slower. Banks can now check if these small businesses can repay loans using government registration systems and GST records, which is why lending to them is taking off.
sibc.implication: A bank should invest in systems that pull data from UDYAM registration and GST records to approve small business loans quickly, because these borrowers are growing much faster than larger ones.
chain: Government systems like UDYAM registration and GST records now give banks reliable data on tiny businesses that were previously invisible
Banks can now check if these small borrowers can repay loans, so they feel safe lending to them
Small business lending grows much faster than lending to bigger businesses because banks finally have the data they need
sibc._driver: MSME formalisation via UDYAM registration + GST data

sibc.title: PLI anchor-supplier chain finance
sibc.body: The government is paying big manufacturers to expand factories under the PLI scheme, and those manufacturers need more parts and materials from smaller suppliers. Lending to suppliers in basic metals grew 16.65 pct and 19.91 pct, chemicals grew 20.6 pct and 19.36 pct, and engineering grew 34.51 pct. These suppliers are safer bets because they have contracts with large established companies that guarantee they will get paid.
sibc.implication: Banks should lend to suppliers who have contracts with PLI manufacturers, especially in engineering where growth hit 34.51 pct, because the big buyer guarantees payment and reduces the risk of default.
chain: Government pays big manufacturers to build new factories under PLI, so those manufacturers need more suppliers for metals, chemicals, and parts
Suppliers get contracts from these big manufacturers, which means guaranteed orders and payment, making them safer to lend to
Banks can grow their loan book quickly by lending to these suppliers, with lower risk because the big manufacturer stands behind the payment

sibc.title: PSLC market depth from PSL reclassification surplus
sibc.body: Housing loans that count toward priority sector lending jumped 39.94% year-over-year after RBI raised the ceiling in October 2024. Banks did not lend more money; the rule change simply let them reclassify existing housing loans as priority sector loans. This created instant surplus PSL credits that banks can now sell to other banks who are short on their required priority sector targets.
sibc.implication: A bank should buy priority sector lending certificates from banks with surplus credits, because it is cheaper and faster than originating new priority sector loans to meet regulatory targets.
chain: RBI raised the housing loan ceiling that counts toward priority sector lending in October 2024
Banks reclassified existing housing loans under the new rule, creating a 39.94% jump in reported priority sector housing loans without any new lending
Banks with surplus priority sector credits can now sell certificates to banks that are short, creating a liquid market for meeting regulatory requirements
sibc._driver: RBI PSL housing loan ceiling revision (Oct 2024)

sibc.title: Renewable energy project finance — next capex wave
sibc.body: Old infrastructure lending is falling fast — some sectors dropped -44.06% and -26.03% this year — while renewable energy sectors jumped 74.02% and 21.47%. Banks already have teams that know how to lend for big, long-term projects like highways and power plants, and those same skills work for solar, wind, and green hydrogen projects. Government renewable energy targets mean these new projects now need the same kind of financing that old infrastructure used to get.
sibc.implication: Move your project finance team from shrinking old infrastructure sectors into renewable energy lending, using the same credit skills and long-term loan structures you already know.
chain: Big infrastructure projects built over the past few years are now repaying loans instead of borrowing new money, so those sectors are shrinking
Government has set big renewable energy targets, creating demand for long-term project loans in solar, wind, and hydrogen
Banks can use their existing project finance skills to lend in these fast-growing renewable sectors instead of waiting for old infrastructure to recover
sibc._via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: Services sector credit entry / scale-up
sibc.body: Services sector lending is growing at 18.58% per year and now represents 28.21% of all bank credit. Banks are increasingly lending through NBFCs that serve services companies — this channel grew 16.68% and now handles 57.1% of the flow. Three services sub-sectors are taking off fast: one is growing at 52.75%, another at 40.7%, and a third at 27.73% per year.
sibc.implication: The bank should expand its NBFC partnerships that focus on services lending and build direct capabilities in the three fastest-growing services sub-sectors to capture this 18.58% annual growth opportunity.
chain: Services companies need more credit and banks are meeting that demand partly by lending to NBFCs that specialize in services
NBFCs now handle 57.1% of services credit flow because they understand these businesses better and can move faster than traditional banks
Three specific services sub-sectors are growing extremely fast (52.75%, 40.7%, and 27.73%) creating a clear opportunity for banks that can lend directly or through the right NBFC partners
sibc._driver: NBFC credit channel recovery post risk-weight cycle

sibc.title: EV-specific vehicle credit products
sibc.body: RETIRED at first FOUNDATION (2026-06-10). No external source verified for EV share of vehicle loans or EV-specific credit risk differentiation. No L1 signal separating EV from ICE vehicle loans. Returns only when EV share of vehicle loans is measurable in L1 (e.g., VAHAN registration data ingestion).
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.

sibc.title: Infrastructure repayment wave with insufficient new origination pipeline
sibc.body: Banks over-indexed to traditional infrastructure credit face a structural squeeze: the FY22-24 project book moves to repayment (compressing balances) while the next-cycle pipeline (renewables, data centres) is slower to ramp. Banks without next-cycle project finance capability face credit portfolio contraction.
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: As a project cohort moves from construction (drawdown) to operations (repayment), net credit balance declines unless new origination offsets it — suppressing growth even while existing books remain large.
This is being driven by Infrastructure supercycle completing (FY22–24).
sibc._via: Project lifecycle phase → new origination vs repayment

sibc.title: CIC reporting compliance gaps creating off-balance-sheet credit risk
sibc.body: RBI Digital Lending Guidelines (2022/2025) require BNPL and digital credit to report to Credit Information Companies. However, compliance may be incomplete among newer digital lenders operating under first-loss guarantee structures or co-lending arrangements where the regulated entity's on-book exposure is partially shielded. If reporting gaps exist, the true unsecured credit stress is understated in bank balance sheets and in SIBC data.
sibc.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Higher risk weights raise the regulatory capital cost per unit of exposure, compressing origination economics for unsecured retail products until reversed.
This is being driven by RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023).
sibc._driver: RBI risk-weight hike on unsecured credit (Nov 2023)
sibc._via: Regulatory capital weight → unsecured origination economics

atm_pos.title: Credit card penetration deepening — PSB share gap is the opening
atm_pos.body: India has 119436129.0 credit cards growing at 8.15 percent per year. Private banks own 71.01 percent of all cards, while public sector banks hold only 23.8 percent. Public sector banks already have millions of debit card customers who trust them and know them, but those customers don't have credit cards yet.
atm_pos.implication: Public sector banks should offer credit cards to their existing debit card customers, because they already have the relationship and the customer data to lend safely.
chain: Public sector banks have a huge base of debit card customers who already bank with them and trust them
These customers need credit cards but are currently going to private banks because public banks aren't offering enough cards
Public banks can grow fast by simply giving credit cards to people they already know, instead of letting private banks take that business

atm_pos.title: Rural cash distribution via Micro ATM / Business Correspondent networks
atm_pos.body: There are 1367989.0 micro ATMs in rural India today, down -7.17 percent from last year. These machines are the cheapest way to get cash to remote villages where building full branches or ATMs costs too much. Fewer machines means fewer people can get cash easily.
atm_pos.implication: The bank should invest in more micro ATMs or partner with business correspondents to expand rural cash access before competitors fill the gap.
chain: Micro ATMs are shrinking by -7.17 percent even though villages need cash
Fewer machines mean rural customers struggle to access banking and may switch to competitors who serve them better
Banks that add micro ATMs now can lock in loyal rural customers before others do

atm_pos.title: Embedded transit and tap-to-pay payment products
atm_pos.body: Credit card spending in the 'other' category jumped 492.78% in value and 299.83% in volume this year. People are tapping their cards to pay for metro rides, bus tickets, and quick store purchases—things they used to pay cash or debit for before. New tap-to-pay systems at metro gates and shop counters turned credit cards into an everyday payment tool.
atm_pos.implication: The bank should issue more credit cards and partner with transit systems and small merchants to install tap-to-pay terminals, because customers now want to use credit cards for daily small purchases.
chain: New tap-to-pay terminals appeared at metro stations and small shops, letting people use credit cards for rides and quick buys
Customers switched from cash and debit to credit cards for these everyday payments because tapping is faster and easier
Credit card spending in this category exploded, showing a huge new market for card issuers and payment infrastructure
atm_pos._driver: Embedded card payment infrastructure rollout (transit NFC or RuPay-credit-on-UPI)
atm_pos._via: Embedded payment rails → new card-spend surface

atm_pos.title: Credit card market concentrated in top-5 private banks
atm_pos.body: The top-5 private banks control approximately 71% of credit card outstanding. The largest single issuer holds ~20% of the market. This concentration creates systemic fragility — as demonstrated when a single payment bank's exit caused a market-wide debit card step-down. Similar disruption from a major credit card issuer would have proportionally larger effects.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.

atm_pos.title: Debit card base structural decline reducing cash access for underbanked
atm_pos.body: The debit card base is contracting through payment bank exits and lifecycle purges. This reduces the potential transaction base for populations that depend on debit cards for cash access and digital payments — particularly lower-income and rural segments without credit cards or reliable UPI-linked accounts.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Regulatory wind-down of a major issuer forces cancellation of its entire card portfolio, producing a single-period step-down in the stock concentrated in that issuer's lender category.
This is being driven by RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026).
atm_pos._driver: RBI enforcement action against Paytm Payments Bank (Jan–Apr 2026)

atm_pos.title: Payment acceptance bifurcation: POS contracting while UPI grows
atm_pos.body: POS terminal network contraction while UPI QR expands creates an acceptance gap: merchants who drop POS terminals cannot accept international cards, EMI transactions, premium credit card features, or POS-authenticated payments. Customers without UPI-linked accounts face reduced options at merchants who have switched fully to UPI.
atm_pos.implication: Move on this while the trend is still running in your favour.
chain: Zero MDR on UPI makes QR acceptance structurally cheaper than card POS (no hardware, no per-txn fee), driving QR deployment while suppressing POS-terminal investment. Polarity is per-target: + for QR, − for POS.
This is being driven by Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI).
atm_pos._driver: Zero MDR policy on UPI transactions (NPCI/GoI)
